_readme: Author-editable enrichment file. See docs/AUTHORING.md. Each key under 'features' is a feature id (visible in the atlas — enable ?ids=1 to see live). Fields: extraFacts[] appended to notes.facts; images[{url,caption,credit}]; yourNotes (string).

extraFacts: Longest river of Rajasthan (~1024 km within the state).
One of the cleanest large rivers in India — habitat for the endangered gharial and the Ganges river dolphin.
The National Chambal Sanctuary (across Rajasthan, MP, UP) protects it as a Ramsar-eligible riverine ecosystem.
Largest tributary of the Chambal and the longest river entirely within Rajasthan.
Sources in the Khamnor hills (Rajsamand) and joins the Chambal downstream of Sawai Madhopur.
Bisalpur Dam on the Banas is the primary drinking-water source for Jaipur, Ajmer and Tonk.
Only major river of the Marwar desert region — 'salt-river' after its brackish downstream reach.
Rises in the Pushkar range (Ajmer), disappears into the Rann of Kutch — never reaches the sea.
Called Sagarmati in its upper reach; becomes 'Luni' (Sanskrit lavana = salt) below Balotra.
One of only two Indian rivers that cross the Tropic of Cancer twice (the other is Mahanadi).
Anchors the Vagad tribal belt — Mahi Bajaj Sagar is Rajasthan's largest hydel outside the Chambal cascade.
Flows through the Beneshwar confluence (Mahi-Som-Jakham) — Rajasthan's biggest tribal fair.
Artificial freshwater lake commissioned by a Banjara chief in 1362, expanded under Maharana Udai Singh in 1560.
Houses Jag Mandir and Jag Niwas (now Taj Lake Palace) on its islands.
Basin: Berach → Chambal. Fed by the Sisarma river and monsoon runoff from surrounding hills.
Built 1687, rebuilt by Maharana Fateh Singh 1889 after a great flood.
Nehru Garden island houses a solar observatory.
Overflow spills over three concrete gates when Udaipur receives good monsoon — a city celebration.
Aridisols — sandy loam with low organic matter (<0.5%), high pH (8-9).
Suited to drought-hardy crops: bajra, moth, guar. Needs sub-surface irrigation for wheat / cotton.
Erosion hazard: severe wind erosion; dunes stabilised by khejri (Prosopis cineraria) shelterbelts.
Vertisols — self-mulching clay derived from Deccan-trap basalt weathering.
Highly retentive of moisture — supports rain-fed soybean and gram in the Hadoti plateau.
Cracks deeply in summer; wheat, cotton and soybean rotate here without irrigation.
Entisols/Inceptisols — river-borne sediments of the Banas, Chambal and Yamuna sub-basins.
Highly fertile; supports the wheat-mustard rotation of the eastern plains + Bharatpur-Alwar belt.
Requires organic manure to sustain productivity under intensive cropping.
BWh in the Köppen scheme — precipitation <300 mm, evaporation >1500 mm.
Covers ~60% of Rajasthan's land area, the largest arid region in India.
Diurnal temperature range often exceeds 20°C — hot summer days, cold winter nights.
BSh in the Köppen scheme — rainfall 300-700 mm; monsoon-dependent.
Transition zone between the Thar (west) and sub-humid Aravalli (south-east).
Supports bajra, gram, mustard and pulses; the state's largest rain-fed cropping belt.
Rainfall so low that the Thar functions as India's only 'true' desert (Köppen BWh).
Aravalli Supergroup — 2.5-1.8 billion years old, one of the world's oldest fold-mountain systems.
Hosts India's largest zinc-lead reserves (Rampura Agucha, Zawar) and marble belt.
Includes granites and phyllites; the range peaks at Guru Shikhar (1722 m).
Meso- to Neo-proterozoic sedimentary basin (~1600-600 Ma).
Rajasthan portion hosts Kota Stone (limestone), sandstone (Dholpur), and building stones.
The Vindhyan sandstones are the source of the pink stones used in Fatehpur Sikri and Delhi's Red Fort.
Ediacaran-Cambrian (~630-542 Ma) sedimentary sequence covering western Rajasthan.
Bilara Group hosts Rajasthan's cement-grade limestone (Jodhpur, Nagaur).
Jodhpur Group is the source of red sandstone used in Umaid Bhawan Palace and Mehrangarh.
Rajasthan holds 100% of India's zinc-lead reserves — Aravalli SG hosts the Rampura Agucha and Zawar deposits.
Hindustan Zinc Ltd (Vedanta) mines all the state's zinc — India's only zinc producer.
Rampura Agucha alone accounts for ~40% of India's zinc-lead production.
Rajasthan supplies ~90% of India's marble; Makrana marble is the world-benchmark white variety.
Ambaji-Kumbalgarh belt has been quarried since antiquity — the Taj Mahal used Makrana.
Rajsamand-Nathdwara area produces green marble; Kishangarh area produces beige.
Largest cultural region of Rajasthan — anchored on Jodhpur, the former Rathore capital.
Marwari dialect and cuisine (dal-baati-churma) are the region's cultural markers.
Godwar is a distinct sub-region at the Aravalli piedmont — Ranakpur temple + Jawai reservoir.
Home to the world's largest open-air fresco gallery — painted havelis of Nawalgarh, Mandawa, Fatehpur.
Historic birthplace of the Marwari trading diaspora — Birla, Bajaj, Poddar, Goenka, Jhunjhunwala.
Distinctive dialect (Shekhawati) — a variant of Marwari with Braj influence.
Broke the ₹3/kWh solar tariff barrier in the 2017 auction, hitting ₹2.44/kWh.
Ownership shared across ~10 solar power developers (SBG Cleantech, Acme, ReNew, Adani, Azure, Hero Future Energies).
RAPS-1 was India's second nuclear reactor (after Tarapur), with design help from Canada (BARC/AECL).
Uses Rana Pratap Sagar reservoir water for cooling — hence the site name Rawatbhata.
RAPS 7-8 (700 MW × 2 indigenous PHWRs) under construction — will take Rawatbhata past 2.5 GW.
One of India's four Pakistan-border districts (~228 km border segment).
Highest 2001-2011 population growth in Rajasthan (32.5%) — driven by oil-and-gas exploration.
Home to India's largest lignite-fired power station (Bhadresh, 1080 MW) and the country's first refinery with captive Rajasthan crude (HRRL Pachpadra, under construction).
India's first planned pre-modern city — founded 1727 by Sawai Jai Singh II following Vastu principles.
Only Rajasthan district with >50% urbanisation (52.4% — Census 2011).
UNESCO World Heritage listed walled city (2019). Two Municipal Corporations after the 2019 split (Greater + Heritage).
Lowest population density in Rajasthan (17/km²) — among the lowest in India.
Largest district by area (38,401 km²).
Home to Rajasthan's only 'living fort' — thousands of residents still live inside Jaisalmer Fort walls.
Highest literacy in Rajasthan (76.56%) — the coaching-education economy is a major driver.
Only non-Jaipur district with >60% urbanisation.
Anchors India's fertiliser + chemical industry via Chambal Fertilisers (Gadepan) and DCM Shriram.
Highest ST share in Rajasthan (76.4%) — the Bhil tribe dominates the district demography.
Anchors the Vagad Fifth-Schedule Scheduled Area under the Constitution.
Mahi Bajaj Sagar Dam provides both hydel power (140 MW) and irrigation for the tribal belt.
Highest SC share in Rajasthan (36.6%) — canal-command labour communities.
'Grain bowl' of Rajasthan — wheat, mustard, kinnow, cotton driven by the IGNP + Gang Canal.
Bounded by Punjab (north) and Pakistan (west) — Rajasthan's northernmost district.
Lowest sex ratio in Rajasthan (846 F/1000M) — chronic skew in the Chambal Ravines belt.
Chambal Ravines / Beehad — India's most extensive badlands, once a dacoit stronghold.
Source of the red sandstone used in Agra Fort and the Red Fort of Delhi.
Highest sex ratio in Rajasthan (994 F/1000M) — tribal belt female-labour participation.
Second-highest ST share (70.8%) after Banswara.
Least urbanised district (6.1%) — subsistence agriculture in southern Aravalli valleys.
